commentBody: Thanks to the incredible tech team at NYT Games I can now do the Tuesday puzzle on Monday morning! (Actually, because I want to do this puzzle at the right time etc. I did not read the column or take a look at the puzzle). Unfortunately the same time travel element here in Games is not available for the business section. I was not able to make a killing on pork futures. No doubt this comment will be erased when the Times resets. Naturally the emus will blame the rheas for the error.
Chewy for a Tuesday puzzle.  Well done.

Congrats on your debut! What a great bucket list item to cross off!
I’ll be honest, not my favOURite theme ever, a little bland even for a Tuesday—but a solid grid and well executed, so kudos on that!!

Fun puzzle, slightly tougher than usual for a Tuesday.  Theme was okay but sort of a neutral presence in the puzzle.

My last square was START/TILL, and honestly I don't understand either of these answers.  I don't get how START is an answer to START or how TILL is a tens or ones place.  T was the only letter that made sensical words there so I guessed it right, but do not understand those clues.

Nice clue with ONE G, with those letters I would have expected an O NEG clue about blood types.

Also OGEE gave me some guff when I originally guessed ESTA instead of ESTE.

As someone born and bred in Ipswich, England, it filled me with joy to see my hometown mentioned (for the first time?) in the NYT crossword today!

For what it’s worth, if you’re looking for ice cream in Ipswich, my go-to was always the shop I-Scream.
Good Tuesday.  I liked how the O-U-R’s led to “Oh You Are…”
I wonder if you can get a Chipwich in Ipswich.  (Maybe in the one in Massachusetts.)
It's not just British spelling... it's also Canadian!
Great debut, John!  A really fun and clever puzzle. Looking forward to your next one.

Two thoughts about specific clues and answers:

14A: Is this way of cluing CIS more acceptable to the intolerant than the other way it has been clued lately?  (It was not obvious to me the way it was clued this time.)

61D: Are the clue and answers both singular, or both plural? (I think the correct answer here is “Yes.”)

A great START for my Tuesday. Finally a correctly spelt puzzle. 

Thanks to the setter and congrats on the debut.
Well, colour me maple-flavoured, neighbour, but actually I’m Canadian. 🇨🇦

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
I look forward to someone pointing out what clue-fill combo in today's grid might trigger potential cruel behaviors and lead to heated debate and invective-laden posts the way "Cut of pork or lamb/CHOP" did yesterday.

15A's "Leer at/OGLE" and 27D's "___asada/CARNE" (call back!) seem like a promising parlay bet to me.
No complaints with any of the answers today 😊

O (Mr. Ruff,) U R now a constructor!
Congrats on your debut and welcome!

It's lovely that you're enjoying your retirement wisely, traveling, spending time with family, doing crosswords, and now constructing. Very inspiring!

For my whole life, every time I've written "color" or "favor" I've been tempted to write them with O-U-R. I always have the impulse, hesitate a split second, and then consciously stop short of following through.

Alas, I would just be considered a fake or a snob since my English, as everyone knows, is American. But I simply find that the added U rounds out the words, making them visually so much more attractive! I've learned to live with the unfulfilled impulse, but if only Dear Abby would make sense of it for me...Or Freud.

Thank you, Mr. Ruff, for turning me into a kid in a candy store, plunking in all the O-U-Rs I've always longed for!

As to the FLAVOUROFTHEDAY and cross-cultural differences in language, if you're ever in California and want to partake in some delicious ice cream, you should know that New York's fountain treats may have different terminology on the West Coast. 

Here's an unauthorized lexicon:
In New York, an "ice cream soda" is soda (let's say root beer) with a scoop of ice cream in it (maybe also some flavored syrup--I'm not sure).
One of my favorite soda fountain treats is New York is a "float," which is a milk shake with an extra scoop of ice cream "floating" in it. If I order a float in California, I get an ice cream soda, as described above--a disappointment that only happened once. 

There are other confusing menu terms...I'm at a loss now. Except one thing I can tell you, if you sit down in a restaurant in California, the wait staff will "always" bring coffee with the menus, hamburgers "always" arrive with lettuce and tomatoes and usually with mustard and mayonnaise (no thanks from me for the sauce--give me my Heinz 57).

And let's not get into sneakers/tennis shoes and height/heighth.

I won't even bring up the topic of egg creams...you can forget about that.
Ugh.
Re the column, I don't think ogee is quite crosswordese. It is a common answer but not rare. If you have ever done any remodeling, picture framing, or furniture repair, you will encounter an ogee.

Only city dwellers eat chervil or chives. On the outskirts of town they eat sub-herbs. 

(This is a joke i made up to appease our American comrades, in case they’re feeling trampled on today.  It doesn’t work in English English. Not sure about Canadian!)

Of course the answer to 58 across should be: “because that is how those words are correctly spelt”.  Unfortunately, that doesn’t quite fit. Two people divided by a common language and all that.  And yes, I still chuckle when my American friends refer to wearing “khaki pants”!
Growing up I was taught there are two kinds of people in the world. There are the British and there are foreigners.
As an American living in England, I enjoyed this.

The first time in a NYT crossword that my knowledge of Renaissance music has been helpful! Surely Palestrina must be next.

Delightful.
I got the whole puzzle but I must confess that when I STARTED it, the 1A clue made me exclaim: "What the HELL?" Took a couple of crosses to get it, but I did. Very clever, indeed.

Why I had to second guess 64A:

A wonderful bird is the pelican,
Its beak can hold more than its belly can.
It ca hold in its beak 
Enough for a week.
I wonder how the hell he can.

41A: "erstwhile" is not "quaint", it is very much in current usage. 
68A: Just because something exists doesn't mean it's present. Mount Rushmore exists, but I can assure you it is not present in my living room right now.

Fantastic debut. What a great idea for a themed Tuesday! 

Congrats!
Thanks, Mr Ruff, for a fun puzzle. As a newbie, I enjoyed solving today! Hope to see more puzzles from you soon.

Why yes, yes I am thank you.
At last, a crossword with the correct spelling *ducks for cover*.
A thoroughly enjoyable debut. I was intrigued at 17A to see where we were going with this, didn’t take long to work it out.

I wanted First for 1A, couldn’t work out what divan could solve to; I only know that term for a bed, so TIL it’s also a SOFA.

 Also TIL the fascinating tidbit re pelicans. I once encountered one on a deserted beach in Florida. Big, aren’t they?  It brought to mind the final scene in Jurassic Park as they were flying out. I clutched my small children and tried not to think of Raptors.  We don’t have scary animals in the UK.

I’m being careful what I write here; the emus have sabotaged my posts for the last week, no idea what or why. Fingers crossed I’m not merely talking to myself. Although I do. I think it’s an age thing.
Spanish for this is either esto or esta.  Este is these.  Or Italian.

Absolutely spiffing puzzle.

Redolent of a First Division table of the mid-70s. Ipswich, Derby, Manny - I can almost picture Don Revie and Brian Clough snarling at each other...

Minor, but este/esta/ese/esa in place of esto/eso always irritates me - you can't randomly pick a gendered form!

Mr. Ruff, 

I thoroughly enjoyed your debut puzzle!

There was an air of charm and freshness
in your clueing,
and after reading your note
I understand why.

Thank you so very much.
A clever and fun debut from someone in an age cohort that I hope I reach.  What's not to like?  Well done and many thanks.
Nice puzzle, lovely for a Chewsday :)

Caught in the middle, I am, spelling things sometimes ‘American’ and sometimes ‘British’. A case of either our.


Nice Tuesday puzzle, with a few clues that had me saying “erm” (which I think rhymes with “what the?”).

A very agreeable Tuesday puzzle. Congratulations on your debut, Mr Ruff. As every Moody Blues fan knows, Every Good Boy Deserves Favour. I think the Brits, Canadians and Aussies commenting are worried for no reason about those Us. We can deal. Today's picture above the column is very of the moment. It's from an article posted within the last few days about Maya Lin's work at the new JP Morgan Chase building in MidTown. Here's a unlocked link if you're interested in reading about it. https://www.nytimes.com/2026/05/10/arts/design/maya-lin-manhattan-skyscraper.html?unlocked_article_code=1.h1A.VSij.uuaTL0ohhqPL&smid=url-share

The circles really seemed silly here, suggesting the possessive "our" rather than the cute phonetic OH YOU ARE of the revealer.

Decent fill and solid enough theme. No circles needed.

In honour of our British friends, eh?

Having lived in Britain and in Canada, I easily slide between spellings and don't really think about it.

'Twas an easy puzzle for me.
Fun puzzle!

My first introduction to the term "sackbut" was its usage in the Cohen Brothers film, The Ladykillers. Such a good movie with the quirkiest of characters. I particularly loved Irma P. Hall (leff my wallet in ElSegundo. All that hippity-hop!)

Today’s theme seems weirdly duplicative of yesterday’s — in each case, the answer to the revealer is a spoke phrase that begins with “Oh”, which calls back to a circled sub-sequence of letters in the other theme answers.

On top of that, I enjoyed yesterday’s theme much more…but I’ve gotta admit, I did find the revealer answer in today’s puzzle surprisingly satisfying.

What a lovely puzzle. We Canadians tend to use the British spellings, so that made it fun. Such clever wordplay.

Hip, hip, hooray, Mr. Ruff!
One g is acceleration (or gravitational field intensity), not force.  The force holding you on Earth is your weight.

Well, that was fun! Just saying the word "sackbut" is almost as fun as listening to one! Here's a rather angry Neptune, accompanied by a quartet of them: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Pz-ezun8cCg&list=RDPz-ezun8cCg&start_radio=1 (From Jean-Pierre Ponelle's brilliant production of Claudio Monteverdi's *Il ritorno d'Ulisse in Patria*, Nikolaus Harnoncourt conducting.) Certainly, today's puzzle will elicit many comments from our Canadian neighbours--or are they neighbors? Don't be too sure. Here's a quiz, offered by the Language Portal of Canada: https://nos-langues.canada.ca/en/quiz/are-you-an-adviser-or-advisor-eng Neither I nor my Canadian Partner aced this, btw.

Fun puzzle, wonderful revealer!

Nice to throw a sop to our friends across the pond. Now I expect a puzzle theme based on Polish spelling for all our friends in Warszawa.


(And I hope you like JAM IN, too)
I found the answer “keens” for the clue “wails” interesting.  Never knew that keens was a Welsh derived word for a high pitched cry.  Learned something new from this puzzle.
Loverly!

Congratulations on a delightful Tuesday puzzle and impressive debut!  

Like Elie, I briefly was stuck in Ipswich, Mass, daydreaming about the scrumptious belly clams at Woodman's and wondering if they have devised a clam-flavored (flavoured?) ice cream.
Fun puzzle ! Streak reached 2400 today ( 6.5 years ) ! Does the TIMES track streaks ? Is there a Guiness record for longest ? Just wondering . Maybe I'm getting there -lol .

Fun Tuesday puzzle. Typical slow start for me, but finally tumbling to the trick was the big turning point and that's always a nice touch. And I thought the reveal was just really clever. Two thumbs up.

And... of course a puzzle find today. I'll put that in a reply.

....

Fun puzzle with a sneaky theme. My BRITISH is not the best but once I got the “OUR” it helped my with my Britishisms… 

Like a DODO, I really struggled with the NW because, although I wanted 14A to be “tanker” it didn’t fit so I assumed it would be “liner” (even though that didn’t seem appropriate). That messed everything up for me. 😝 

Fun puzzle! Not too difficult but appropriate for a Tuesday!

Rating:
9/10

Is this Circles Week?

Ahem.  Ogden Nash has spoken, and we say the pelican's "BEAK can hold more than his bellican."  

Further:  people in debt are NOT "swimming."  Too late for that!  They are DROWNING.

Psych!  The 61D clue mentions "Wapiti"--didn't I just mention that name?  We are experiencing a herd of ELK in Crosslandia....

Aaaaand I got DOOKed at 66A, wondering how a blood type was affecting our gravitational status.....
Congratulations, John! A truly delightful debut.

I would have liked this better if there hadn't been any tiny little circles for the theme answers.  The themers would have been a bit harder and the revealer -- where the tiny little circles are what make it sing -- would have come as a MUCH bigger and better surprise.  But a cute puzzle anyway, if rather easy.

Nice Tuesday puzzle.
More than a few clues that put up a bit of resistance (ABDUCTORS, OUTBREAKS, JAM IN, DEBT), and a solid Tuesday theme with a charming and satisfying revealer.

And I'll but a Czech-mark next to any puzzle that includes Franz KAFKA.

Nice job, John Ruff.  Congratulations on your debut!
And, from you short bio, it sounds like you are living a wonderful life!
In Spanish "this" has three forms: masculine, feminine, and neutral. Do better with your hints.

I once had a student who submitted a paper that was filled with British spellings -- -our- and -ise -- and I called her into my office to ask about this.
"Well, it is the *English* language, you know." she responded.
I told her that in OUR country, they were misspellings and asked her to re-write it.

To this day, I don't know if she was plagiariSing or just pretentious.

A fun and clever puzzle! Very satisfying that O-U-R British was used so neatly as the revealer!!! Need to file OGEE away for future arch-related questions...it was there somewhere in the back of my brain, so helped me with the Spanish article, which, as somone just pointed out, could end with one of three letters. 

Been away from the crossword for awhile...we are fostering-to-adopt a little puppy from our local shelter. He came with a broken leg, so that has been taking most of my attention. What a delightful puzzle to come back to; hope for more from John Ruff!

HA! I just looked up the author of this puzzle to write the above, and I am chuckling that the author's name is so befitting of my dog-oriented world these days!! 🤣
Huh, I'm wearing an Under Armour sweatshirt, and it just occurred to me that an American manufacturer was using the British spelling. Blimey!

Several posters have cited the limerick, often attributed to Ogden Nash: "A wonderful bird is the pelican, Its beak can hold more than its bellican. It ca hold in its beak Enough for a week. I wonder how the hellican." (or some variant thereof.) "Guess what?," he politely asks, "Nash didn't write it!" It was first published in 1913, in the Florida Tribune--which described it as already "a classic–one which merits conspicuous publication in all the high-class literary journals," and attributes it to one C. M. Mars(h)to, an editor at the Chicago Record-Herald. Who knew pelicans were such long-distant migrants? In any case, long before Nash. https://quoteinvestigator.com/2020/06/20/pelican/ Know what Nash did write? "There goes the wapiti, Hippety-hoppity!" (New Yorker, 1933)
Thank you, John Ruff, for a very enjoyable puzzle. Much appreciated this side of the pond!

And one more puzzle find. A diagramless from Jan 22, 2012 by Fred Piscop. The theme answers all had 'Dog' as part of the clue. Here are some of the answers, with the clues. "Dog's Monopoly property?" BARKPLACE "Dog's submarine group?" WOOFPACK And some other theme answers: RUFFCUTDIAMOND ARFWIEDERSEHEN YAPSTORE Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Variety?date=1/22/2012&g=67&d=A ....

Thank you, John, for this lovely debut and delightful theme with its flavourful nod to our UK friends!🇬🇧

I hope we see you again soon and that you and your wife continue to enjoy a well-deserved retirement -- hats off to our educators!😊
Good job John – I really liked it. It must feel great to be published in the NYT. Alas, I'll probably never achieve that, but it's fun to try anyway (and dream).
Loverly!

The three place names could also have been Charlottetown, Thunder Bay, and Moose Jaw as well.  Canada also uses the "our" spellings.  (But I'm guessing Ipswich, Derby and Manchester are more well-known.)

And... another puzzle find. A Thursday from October 24, 2019 by Matt Ginsberg. The first theme clue and answer... "When prefixed with 72-across, what a friend wishes for you" BUTTHEBEST And... there was no 72-across in the puzzle. Some other similarly clued theme answers: TOSNEEZEAT TOWEAR UPMYSLEEVE LEFTBEHIND TOIT BUTNET Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=10/24/2019&g=57&d=A ....

Quite enjoyable!

Somewhat off topic. 

Was reading an article this morning and a guy in Ohio was quoted using the phrase Cutting even where I would have said Breaking even.   Wondering if this is common, a Midwestern thing or just this guy? 

Is this familiar to anyone?
So now I wonder - if I travel from the US to the UK and and text someone from my smart phone does autocorrect add a 'u' in words like colo(u)r?  Does it replace the 'z' in organize with an 's'?

To those who piled on the "chop" comment, this is what I heard:  

Quick! Quick! Cover your ears! LA-LA-LA-LA-LA-LA.  Mommy, I'm scared. He thinks different from me.
It was nice when the important boxes lit up in bright yellow, adding pzazz to the presentation.

Hi solvers! So glad you enjoyed John Ruff's puzzle. This was a fun one to solve and write about. A small announcement: I'm working on an article about streaks. Breaking them, keeping them, using them to stay in touch with your loved ones — I want to hear your stories about them. If you've ever done anything drastic to keep your streak, or simply have strong feelings about them, fill out this form. I'm looking forward to reading your stories and I may reach out to ask for more info. All the key details can be found here: https://nyti.ms/4nx7yJZ
Well, I mean . . .
"A fun and spirited Tuesday solve" I couldn't agree with you more!
Great puzzle today. Thanks

A dad jokes puzzle. A Friday from December 12, 1975 by Dennis Listort. Three 15 letter answers: WHATDIDDELAWARE WHENDIDMARYLAND IDONTKNOWALASKA Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/PS?date=12/12/1975&g=35&d=A ....

As a volunteer editor on the English Wikipedia, we have little template reminders to add to articles that should maintain US, UK, or other English spellings and date orders. Good reminder that "English" is not equal to "United States".

As a Canadian, it was amazing being able to use OUR spelling for once in a NYT puzzle!
Spelling used in UK, Ireland, Australia, New Zealand, Canada, South Africa.
Fun.  Almost adourable.

In the meantime, they've continued rotating the M-W columnists. Sean McGowan is the new regular Thursday and Friday columnist: https://www.nytimes.com/by/sean-mcgowan
@Chris Till for a cash drawer
@Chris because 1A is the START of the puzzle
@Chris START because it’s the first clue in the puzzle if you start in the upper left (which most solvers do).

@FWIW

Which is not short for “until.”

@Joe Ipswich is one of my favorite words - from long before I knew it was a place.
Why? It's a long story. So here it is. There was a Saturday Night Live (or maybe Not Necessarily the News, or both) gag in the early eighties called Sniglets. Sniglets are new made-up words that fulfill modern requirements. One "real" Sniglet I remember is "Aspirfection".  That was the art of popping exactly two aspirin tablets from a bottle onto your hand, instead of clumsily digging or  over pouring.
I was around 12 at this time and invented two Sniglets of my own. One was "gleek" - which is when you accidentallly (or intentionally for some lucky people) squirt saliva directly from your salivary glands out of your mouth as tiny jets. This Sniglet seems to have made it into the modern vocabulary somehow.
My other Sniglet was "Ipswitch", which is any place on the leg seam on your Levis where the dominant or higher fabric piece changes from the lower leg piece to the upper piece. This usually happens once or twice on most Levis.
I've always wanted to visit Ipswich.

@Joe We used to say "I Scream for Ice Cream." BTW, when I visited London twice, I found the ice cream and creams (clotted cream) the BEST ever and I eat a LOT of ice cream (every day). Nothing I ever had in my home country compares to London's!

@MC If you've ever been in a NYC subway, you would have met with the thing I call the skreeker. It makes that sound when it alerts people to incoming trains. To me, a butterfly is a flutterby because that's really what it does. I was always making words up even before that game. There was even a game we bought called Sniglets.
@MC Rich Hall did the sniglets! The one that has stuck with me is “musquirt’” which is the liquid that squirts out of the mustard container if it’s not properly shaken first.
@MC Levis usually have French seams (seam flap is sewed down), so that switching should not happen. I's need a picture to see an Ipswitch. Cute word though.

@MC 
Sniglets were definitely a NNtN feature, created by Rich Hall. He came up with some good ones, but I always thought the term itself was kind of stupid.
@Dean I missed that!

@Steve L You might be more likely to find the chipwich in Sandwich (on Cape Cod) than in Ipswich (near Cape Ann). Incidentally, Cape Ann also has a Manchester, although the locals started calling it "Manchester by the Sea" to differentiate themselves from Manchester NH.
@Dave. That reminds me of a visit we had from a good friend from Connecticut who mused that wasn’t it odd that so many English towns were named after ones in New England!
@Dave. It is also know as MBS, by those in the ara
@Trish … and Australian, New Zealand …

@Patrick J. India, when they write in "English"?

@Steve L
I've been watching a few cop shows lately and they frequently refer to their confidential informants as CIs, so had no trouble getting the reference. But I also have no problem with the other clue being used.

@Steve L

Both of my replies
to both of your thoughts-

1) Both
2) Both


(Now “both” doesn’t sound like a real word.
Both…both. 
Both. Both. Both…)

@Vaer

I don't watch cop shows, so it didn't ring a bell to me.
@Kirsten Aren't Canadians also British? Belongs to Great Britain, no? So, it could well have been spelt out in Montreal or Toronto as well.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight Members of the British Commonwealth at a stretch? But you wouldn't refer to yourself as "North Atlantic" because the US is a part of NATO, and I say this as a proud Nationian of the United Nations

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight

They belong to the Commonwealth, and as such they share the King as a figurehead, but Canada is still an independent country and definitely not part of the United Kingdom.

@Ms. Billie M. Spaight
I’m awaiting official birth certificates for my mother and grandmother and then I will be applying for Canadian citizenship by descent and fairly soon should be able to say that too.

@Matt (bcc@Andrzej)
In the FWIW department, I read that visceral exchange late yesterday and opted to comment in my own space yesterday. As a stand-alone comment, it looked a tad darker than I intended, but meh, it's done. I've seen worse here.
The proselytizer, topic aside, looked to be a polished writer. My admiration of such skillful writing keeps drawing me back to visiting certain commenters. 

My vocabulary has always been quite good, but 35 years in the tech industry made it very engineer-ese. My technical writing and authoring of design documents called for very simple and succinct language, with tons of internet-centric terminology. And, of course, the usual corporate-speak (which I loathe).

So, out of curiosity, what drives your writing style? Do you also speak in such vernacular? It's very poetic in nature, not a terribly easy read, but intriguing, nonetheless.
Thanks!

@Jerry

My spoken English is generally quite plain, as it's much harder to give it any sort of flair in real time when you're speaking a foreign language. But I *do* enjoy a bit of flair, and I occasionally let it show when I'm writing in English. I especially enjoy my prose having a slightly disturbing undercurrent, and words like "moist" work perfectly here.

Now, my Polish is another thing, entirely. I have been educated in the humanities at some of Warsaw's best schools (no worries, that's no proof of obscene wealth or privilege - our best schools are public, and education is financed by the government), I've always been a reader, and I write for work and for fun, too. Thus, I have a vocabulary way above average, and I am a master at what Polish has always been very well suited to - that is, constructing compound-complex sentences dozens of words long. I can also duplicate a range of literary styles, both modern and antiquated, and I have a distinct style of my own. I Φ-ing love my Polish!

As a professor, I have to control how I speak and write when addressing my students, especially the kids fresh out of high school, and so I have learned to use a simple version of my Polish, too.

And finally... My mother - professor, public servant, author, whose funeral was attended by a hundred people, if not more, including not one but two former Presidents - cussed like a sailor. I learned much from her, also her colorful language. I can build a whole story out of profanities :D

@Matt
I had lamb kebab for supper last night, purely out of spite.

userLocation: Spring Hill, just south of Armageddon
commentBody:
@Jerry

I've just always loved writing.

Too narcissistic to be a great writer but nimble enough to express myself well, imitate many styles for fun, write speeches for folks and academic stuff pretty decently when required.

On boards like this I just enjoy riffing, throwing rose petals and sometimes flexing just for the sake of it (see narcissist above) especially when the carcass we share is wordplay and whatever detritus spins off it.

Like veganism and life behind the Iron Curtain for wee Andrzej.

Thanks for the up.

You may have missed Richard's joke

@sotto voce

When I started learning English in the 1980s (and when I was in school in the 80s and 90s), British English was our standard of the language, and it had been for decades: my wife's grandfather learned British English in the mid-20th century, too, as did my mother in the 1960s. This was probably still rooted in the British cultural, economic, and military dominance of the 19th century, and the conviction that British English was the true  original version of the language. American English has become the default only recently, after Poland opened up to the modern world after 1989, and especially in the 2000s, with the rise of the internet. In fact, when I'm writing in English, I still hesitate when I'm skipping the "u" in "flavor" and the like.

@Andrzej

But in your case, because you originally learned British English – as most people in Europe do – it would be perfectly acceptable for you to use O-U-R, even here in this forum. No one would find you pretentious, as they certainly would me, lol.

@sotto voce

Neither British nor American English is "my" language, and neither is better or worse, objectively speaking. With a mostly American audience, like here, I find myself using American spelling. When I post a comment on The Guardian's website I go for British spelling. It's all a bit schizophrenic.

The spelling thing is easy, at least (well,  mostly. I can never remember whether gray or grey is American or British, etc. Math/maths? Sport/sports? I tried brute-force-remembering these but I failed every time). I usually don't know whether expressions and figures of speech are from the UK or US. Recently I learned on this board my favorite acronym of CBA is British 🤷🏽.

(Also, the people who regularly remind me this is an American app for Americans would surely mind it if I used British spelling on top of admitting American trivia often stops me in my tracks).

@Andrzej 
I too struggle with gray/grey

@Andrzej

Grey versus gray used to confuse me, so I came up with this:
With "A" = American
With "E" = (British) English
:-)

@sotto voce

That's good, maybe I'll remember it. Thank you 🙂

@sotto voce
I too think the -our ending looks better--it adds a touch of glamour!

@sotto voce

I spell theatre the British way, -re instead of -er. I don’t care if I look like a snob. I like it better that way.

@Katie
Hah! Masterful with! Love it.

@Katie

*wit

@Heidi

Ah, the occasionally perfect "I don't care" solution.
I should try it more often! 
Thanks for the nudge! ;-)

@Andrzej I was a reading child, and I read a lot of (mainly?) British children's books: Winnie-the-Pooh, Alice in Wonderland, The Wind in the Willows, Peter Pan, The Borrowers, the Narnia books... I could go on and on.  It gives me pleasure just to name them.  From these books I picked up British orthography, and I always preferred the "ou" spellings.  How sophisticated they looked to me!  I don't remember any of my American teachers correcting me, so I use them to this day.  My siblings tease me about it, but when I became an English teacher in a German school, it came in rather handy.  My experience is that British English is still very much preferred by Europeans.

@lucky13
Have you every been to California?

@lucky13 
"Heighth" is not a word, any more than "supposably", "acrossed", "nucular", "athalete", or "realitor". It is not a legitimate variant, it is simply a mispronunciation. People confuse it with constructions such as "length" and "breadth" and "width" - thank you, English language.

@lucky13
I'm with Heidi on what a root beer float is. I grew up on Long Island and on hot afternoons in August, I'd have one as a treat.

@lucky13

As Heidi said, root beer with ice cream in it is a root beer float.  (A Coke float substitutes Coca-Cola for the root beer.) An ice cream soda is made with chocolate or vanilla syrup and seltzer, with a scoop of ice cream (your choice of flavor) in it.  A chocolate soda with vanilla ice cream is called a black and white.  There is no white and black.

An egg cream is the syrup and the seltzer, with a bit of milk mixed in.  Chocolate is the standard, but vanilla is possible.

BTW, vanilla soda is bottled commercially as "cream soda."

Fox's U-Bet is the standard for a NYC chocolate egg cream.

Source:  I was a soda jerk in my grandfather's pharmacy in 1968-69.

@Steve L

And did you turn the spoon backwards when the seltzer was hitting the glass for that egg cream?

@Katie

Wait, so are the pizza pies square, too? I’m having trouble picturing a round object cut into squares. Someone’s getting weird little wedges. And does that also mean there are middle pieces with no crust to use as handles? What’s wrong with South Dakota?

@lucky13
Now see what you've started!?!!

And no one even got into the soda/pop/soft drink/cola thing.
This thing spread like wildfire.
My advice:  change your name and hide!  Putting you on the Prayer List.

@Times Rita

I don't think so.  As I remember, the froth was achieved by forceful stirring during the seltzer pour.

@Heidi
St.Louis-style pizza is also cut in squares. Yes, the pies are round. Yes, that means the middle pieces have no edges to use as handles, but the crust itself is so thin, there really isn’t an edge - the toppings go all the way to the edge. And yes, it means a few pieces are these tiny odd-shaped bites that aren’t squares, but aren’t really wedges either. It was a shock when I first encountered it nearly 40 years ago!
I lived for years in California and have never once received coffee without asking for it.
My Chicago-bred folks called root beer + ice cream a 'black cow'.
@Heidi We used to call the root beer float a brown cow.

@Heidi
Sicilian pies are traditionally square, at least in NY metro areas.

@Heidi
In CT the pizza was cut in squares. And, the crust-less middle pieces were the first to be eaten. Yum.

@NanuNanu

This is what Merriam-Webster says about "heighth":

heighth
noun
ˈhī(t)th
chiefly dialectal variant of height

I noticed in California, a long time ago, that almost everyone from there said "heighth" instead of "height." Weird. 

By the way, I would say that if a lot of people use it, it is a word. What bothers me is if people say "blinder" (to make a smoothie) or Windsday (the day after Tuesday), etc.

Another thing that confused me: painted on the streets near the cross streets was "PED XING." At first, I thought it was Chinese. Nope: Pedestrian Crossing.

Also, I noticed that nobody in California jaywalked. People would wait at the corner patiently even if there was no traffic anywhere visible, and then cross when the light changed. Here in New York, not so much.

Another thing I noticed: almost everyone was blonde, it seemed to me. Today, maybe not so much.

One more thing, almost everyone I met said to me: ""You have a New York accent."

Oh, and rarely, back then did I meet anyone who was born there: now, maybe not so much.

@lucky13
Your California cuisine crit sounds like the Denny's syndrome.  Next time drive into town and expand your experience.
@Dan Candidate for Least Helpful Comment of the Day.

@fionatimes

Crosswordese doesn't have to be something that's unused and obsolete.  It just has to appear more often in the crossword than in real life.  Brian ENO, for example, is a brilliant and accomplished musician and producer, but we don't talk about him once a week unless we know him personally.

@Steve L
After living a good while, and consumed, played and appreciated music that whole time, I have never heard a song by Eno, that I know of.  I no nothing about him. I'm perplexed as to why he shows up so often in the NYT crossword.
@Hanson Eno's album "Here Come the Warm Jets" was a favorite of mine, once upon a time. Here's "Baby's on Fire" from that album: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fbU1zYzD-Tw

@Hanson

You’ve probably heard much more from Brian Eno than you realize. Aside from his own (largely experimental) music, he has produced works for a long list of artists, including Talking Heads, U2, James, Genesis. I first became aware of him through his collaboration with David Bowie on the classic Low, Heroes and Lodger trio of albums. He was also a co-founder of Roxy Music, but left them early on.

Hanson,
Surely (or Shirley) you understand ENO shows up so often because of the letters of his last name. I never saw Cheri OTERI on SNL; I see her here often for the same reason.

@Barry Ancona

Ms. OTERI is currently a recurring guest star on Reba McEntire’s current sitcom, Happy’s Place (Monica the health inspector).

@Hanson

Well, I think everyone else has already covered it...

@Hanson

What Heidi said. Roxy Music in the 70s was my intro to him.  Obviously, ENO is useful three-letter glue with two vowels for Xwords, like ONO. The next step up from black blocks.  For my taste, Eno’s collaborations are much more interesting than his solo “ambient” stuff. (Similar to the background, enabling role his name provides to crosswords.) But, that’s just me.

@Andrzej  It gave a tiny pleasurable thrill to enter "ogee" with confidence.  I only know this word because in my old American Heritage dictionary, it was one of the words accompanied by a picture.  After using that dictionary for years, it stuck. I can still see the pretty little photograph in my mind's eye.

@MExpat

That's how I remember the Polish terms - they featured as descriptions of pictures in architecture handbooks.

Keep this up and soon you'll have a string of Sunday puzzles in the NYT 😐

@Andrzej I have been thinking of trying to construct a puzzle based on the differences between British and American English. I think it would be a hoot but when I’ve tried making a puzzle within the constraints the NYT sets out, I’ve found it impossible. I must give it another go!

@Petrol

No. I've head many dealings with editors over 20 years of publishing papers, and let me tell you, most of them had no idea what they were doing. Not only did they propose pointless stylistic changes, they also introduced factual errors. And I meant it about Kugelman above - the editors at the NYT are the ones who have decided to publish 20 (or so) of *his* puzzles over 2 years! To me, that is no proof of infallible skills and judgment 🤣

@Petrol
I think the American base'll appreciate it -- not completely certain, just parsley sure.
@Petrol My adult kid had a little piece of tissue sticking out of his nose once. Had to tactfully suggest a little nasal followup 🤧

@John

@Patrick J. 

This apparently either was or was not written by Ogden Nash, depending on which source you cite, so all possibilities are covered.  My money now is on "was not," even though I've believed Ogden Nash wrote it ever since I first memorized it in grade school.

@NanuNanu

Crossword clues are not definitions, and the answers don't need to be synonyms of the clues. I get you, and it bugged me, too, but consider this: whatever is physically present also exists (even if all that exists is not necessarily present where I am).

@Andrzej

I had the same reaction as NanuNanu.  Sure, you can explain it away as you did, but if the clue doesn't serve for a sly misdirection (and here, it doesn't), it's just a poorly worded clue.  "Be" would be sufficient, and better, as a clue.

Steve,
Please consider that the reason might well be a Tuesday-level "sly misdirection" (as in we don't have to hear the tree fall in the forest).

commentBody:
@NanuNanu

"Very much in current usage," eh?

In the skilled nursing facility wing of the Yale Club nursing home?

@Matt Well according to Google's ngram site, "erstwhile" is apparently more common now than at any other time in the past 200 years. And while I don't think there's a regulatory authority defining what qualifies as common usage, "erstwhile" now appears in print much more often than "white pages", and I don't think you can argue that that's an obscure phrase. Frequency in 2020: "white pages" - 0.0000001359% "erstwhile" - 0.0000024051% https://books.google.com/ngrams/graph?content=Erstwhile&year_start=1800&year_end=2022&corpus=en&smoothing=3 https://books.google.com/ngrams/graph?content=White+pages&year_start=1800&year_end=2022&corpus=en&smoothing=3

commentBody:
@Andrzej
I’ll give you Reform, what a waste of oxygen. But cows are delightful creatures, endlessly curious. A wave of your arms and a general whoop will send them on their way. The fierce looking Highland Coos are the sweetest. The only ones to avoid is a cow with calf; like any Mum they will protect their own.  Most injuries are sustained by getting too close to a calf. Bullocks can be frisky little blighters too and very heavy if they stand on your foot, as I know to my cost.

@Helen Wright

I know about cows - my father grew up in the country so he taught me much about it, even though I am a city kid - and my wife and I have done enough Alpine hiking to know to not approach a cow (or any animal, really) with its young. These Scottish cows though... Something really felt off. We were both properly spooked. Maybe the wind-swept, secluded location was to blame for what we sensed, by there was a sense of danger in the air. We thought maybe it was milking time, and they approached us to take care of that? But their udders didn't look full, and there was none of the moo-ing I've learned to associate with a cow calling to be milked.

@Helen Wright 

Seems to me this could be an issue of familiarity. As a coastal American, pelicans are fairly commonplace & non-threatening. As for cows, they are reputed to be so dumb it’s hard to imagine them as threatening. Cow tipping is an old-timey prank. Use the term “bull,” however, and an entirely different connotation will arise.

@Andrzej
That does sound weird. Perhaps something had spooked them earlier, like a loose dog perhaps, then you were next on the scene. Either way I’m glad you and your wife escaped unharmed.

@Helen Wright

Yes, my first thought was “just watch your feet.” My second was, “stand to the side, especially when they cough.”

@Helen Wright

That's interesting about "scary animals.".  The closest to an apex predator there is a badger. I guess it's not surprising if an island is inhabited by humans long enough the big predators will go extinct.

I agree pelicans are indeed large at close range, compared to toddlers! As for cows, I grew up in US cow country and found they would come even from far afield curious to see what the humans were up to, then stand and stare expectantly.

@Thomas Ruppel

Wow, you stated that so confidently!  And yet quite wrong.

ESTE and esta are the masculine and feminine forms for "this," respectively:  ESTE libro, esta pluma.

ESTO is neuter and is used without a noun:  ¿Qué es esto?  
Esto es increíble.

Estos and estas are "these."

@Thomas Ruppel

Steve L. already set you straight, but as a retired Spanish teacher, I will allow you to take a make-up test. Also, the Italian equivalent is QUESTO, QUESTA. But thanks for the laugh.

Cliff Elis, I'm not following your distinction. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G-force

@Cliff Elis

Yes, but ONEGM (1 g m) doesn't fit.      ;-)
@Cliff Elis mass

@Barry Ancona I'm making a Physics 101 distinction in units and numeric values.  All objects falling near the surface of the earth experience the same acceleration of 9.8 m/s/s in a vacuum.  But each object experiences a numerically different force -- e.g. the force on me would be 622 kg m/s/s (622 Newtons or 140 lbs) but the force on a baseball would be about 1.4 N =0.315 lb    Note that  both the Wiki page and Elie Levine's commentary point out, a "g" is a force per unit mass.
